dailyBuilds.summary: Seven recorded daily builds completed. No fatal crashes were logged, but model-credit exhaustion caused four recoverable fallback warnings.

highlights.mostSignificantFeature: The Earth crossed from a static globe into a time-aware world: atmosphere, moon, weather, moving clouds, and seasonal biome shifts now make the planet feel alive.
highlights.mostSignificantSetback: The same credit-related model failure pattern appeared four times this week, forcing fallbacks and making the daily builder less predictable.
highlights.health: Healthy visual momentum, but nearing the top of Genesis. The next week should focus on richer shader quality, clearer day-night contrast, and more readable atmospheric motion so complexity 10 feels earned.

recurringIssues.title: Repeated model credit exhaustion
recurringIssues.note: This happened 3+ times this week and should stay prominent until the daily runner prefers lower-cost models or trims token demand.

research.title: Sun-aware atmosphere glow
research.details: Use a separate atmosphere shell with ShaderMaterial, THREE.BackSide, and THREE.AdditiveBlending. Fresnel glow is strongest at the rim and should be mixed with a sun-direction term so the sunlit edge stays blue while the terminator warms toward orange.
research.snippet: float rim = 1.0 - abs(dot(toCamera, vNormal)); rim = pow(rim, 2.8); float sunMix = clamp(dot(normalize(vNormal), normalize(sunDirection)) * 0.5 + 0.5, 0.0, 1.0);
gotchas: Compute normals and sun direction in the same space.
Use a slightly larger sphere for the atmosphere shell.
Keep the atmosphere transparent with depthWrite disabled to avoid halo artifacts.

research.title: Animated cloud shell on a second sphere
research.details: The most practical Genesis-phase upgrade is still a second sphere for clouds. A CanvasTexture or alpha map on SphereGeometry(1.01+) with independent rotation is cheap and convincing; later it can evolve into displaced or shader-driven cloud motion.
research.snippet: const clouds = new THREE.Mesh(new THREE.SphereGeometry(1.018, 64, 64), new THREE.MeshPhongMaterial({ map: cloudTexture, transparent: true, opacity: 0.6, depthWrite: false })); clouds.rotation.y += 0.0012;
gotchas: Set depthWrite to false so cloud transparency does not cut into the Earth.
Keep the cloud sphere only slightly larger than the Earth to avoid visible separation.
Use soft alpha edges or banded streaks so the shell does not look like pasted blobs.

research.title: Soft terminator and city-light blending
research.details: Blend day and night textures from the sun-normal dot product. A logistic curve produces a more natural terminator than a hard clamp and leaves room for twilight tinting and light bloom on the dark side.
research.snippet: float light = dot(normalize(vWorldNormal), normalize(sunDirection)); float mixAmount = 1.0 / (1.0 + exp(-20.0 * light)); vec3 color = mix(nightColor, dayColor, mixAmount);
gotchas: Normalize the sun vector every frame before writing the uniform.
If remote night textures are added later, load them with crossOrigin='anonymous'.
Keep twilight color separate from the night texture so the boundary stays readable.

research.title: Texture and library path for the next phase
research.details: Solar System Scope hosts day, night, cloud, normal, and specular Earth textures under CC-BY 4.0. When the project graduates from purely procedural maps, these can seed a more detailed Living World pass.
gotchas: Match any examples or postprocessing scripts to the same Three.js revision as the core build.
EffectComposer requires its dependent passes in order: RenderPass, ShaderPass, then UnrealBloomPass.
External images should be mirrored locally or loaded with permissive CORS headers.

deliverables: Sun-tinted Fresnel atmosphere on the limb
Cleaner on-screen telemetry for day, complexity, and phase
No regressions in drag rotation or moon orbit
Replace static cloud blobs with softer streaks and bands
Keep cloud shell rotating independently from surface land
Tune transparency so cloud cover reads at a glance
Refine day-night shader transition
Strengthen city-light readability on the dark hemisphere
Add a subtle twilight band instead of a flat edge
Improve ocean glints and night-side contrast
Stabilize HUD and observatory copy
Prepare the codebase for complexity 10 transition
Prototype real weather overlays from Open-Meteo or equivalent free data
Define how external data is cached or gracefully skipped
Preserve render speed while adding live information
Test a normal or specular map path
Evaluate CC-BY Earth texture sources for quality and CORS behavior
Keep a procedural fallback so builds never depend on one external host
Reduce repeated credit-exhaustion warnings in the daily runner
Prefer reliable low-cost model paths at this complexity level
Document the transition from Genesis to Living World in the observatory
